High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Very low openness, high authoritarian submission, and strong system justification drive skepticism toward forcing platform transparency, as it challenges existing power structures and feels like unnecessary interference.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (98.33) and strong privacy/anti-surveillance stance (82.47) support transparency, but very low trust in government (16.53) and institutions tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Very low openness and high conscientiousness plus a dangerous worldview motivate cautious support for transparency as a system-stabilizing reform, but low agreeableness and strong anti-egalitarianism keep enthusiasm from being strong.

High openness and low conscientiousness, combined with strong anti-elitism and a desire for system disruption, drive moderate support for algorithmic transparency as a check on opaque corporate power.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate system justification (81.62%) and trust in institutions balance with strong libertarian anti-state-control views (86.51%), resulting in near-neutral mild support for regulation.
Strong authoritarian submission and high system justification favor holding platforms accountable, reinforced by high dutifulness.

Low trust in government (7.77) and high anti-elitism (52.77) combined with a strong free spirit (67.34) and disruptor (68.94) user type produce mild opposition to regulation, seen as potentially stifling innovation and freedom.
High authoritarian submission and system justification favor existing platform control, while low openness and anti-elitism oppose new regulations seen as elite-driven.

Strong opposition from very low trust in government (16.53) and high anti-elitism (78.31) combined with libertarian skepticism of state control (76.24), though fairness values (89.23) provide slight counterweight.

Authoritarian law-and-order preference and high system justification favor regulation, but low openness and competitive worldview limit enthusiasm for empowering regulatory agencies, producing moderate support.
Mild support reflects a tension between high authoritarianism (favoring regulation) and low trust in government, with a disruptor's skepticism of top-down control.

High skepticism of state control (88.14) and anti-elitism (79.77) create resistance to regulation, but high conventionalism (88.47) and respect for rules (99.48) pull toward accountability, resulting in near-neutral leaning slightly against.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High trust in institutions is outweighed by very strong free-spirit autonomy (99.22%) and low openness to imposed media systems, plus a preference for market-driven or user-led alternatives.
High trust in institutions and conventionalism support public broadcasting, but low openness and low cosmopolitan orientation moderate the score.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and science (2.57) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong competitive worldview (87.64) generate clear opposition to funding a state-backed media entity perceived as an elite institution.
Low trust in institutions and very low openness make public broadcasting seem untrustworthy and unnecessary, though moderate civic nationalism provides slight counterweight.

Very low trust in institutions (6.19) and high anti-elitism (78.31) combined with strong populist people-centrism (65.41) and low trust in government (16.53) drive clear opposition to state-funded media.

Strong anti-government anti-elitism (populist attitudes), collective narcissism, and very low openness make public broadcasting seem like an elite-captured institution, driving clear opposition despite moderate institutional trust.
Very low trust in mainstream institutions and low nationalism produce slight opposition, but high openness and a philanthropic orientation prevent strong rejection.

Moderate trust in government (55.44) and high conventionalism (88.47) support public broadcasting, but strong anti-elitism (79.77) and skepticism of state control (88.14) limit enthusiasm, yielding mild support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong autonomy values (free spirit 87.80%) and very high libertarian impulse (97.84%) drive support for user choice mandates, while low conscientiousness (20.19%) keeps it moderate.
Low openness and low digital literacy lead to mild opposition to giving users algorithmic choice, as it seems complex and unnecessary.

High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.
High disruptor traits and anti-censorship orientation support user choice, but low openness and high authoritarian submission limit enthusiasm for mandated changes.

High free spirit autonomy (68.23) and anti-censorship (98.33) values strongly support user choice, while low conscientiousness (12.08) and low trust in platforms (23.94) add desire for external options.

Low openness and high traditionalism make mandates feel like unnecessary interference with existing norms, while high conscientiousness and moderate system justification keep opposition from being strong.
Moderate-to-clear support is driven by high openness, a disruptor's desire to challenge algorithmic control, and a populist emphasis on user sovereignty, despite low digital literacy.

High free spirit autonomy (60.17) and experimentation (89.16) strongly support user choice, while high conscientiousness (73.59) and moderate trust in experts (58.39) temper the mandate aspect, producing moderate support.

High dutifulness, rule-orientation, and dangerous worldview strongly favor labelling as a clear protective intervention, with low libertarian anti-censorship and moderate authoritarian submission reinforcing support.
Support is moderate, fueled by high threat sensitivity and authoritarian preference for order, but tempered by low trust in the labeling authorities and a libertarian streak.

High conventionalism (88.47) and respect for rules (99.48) combined with low dangerous worldview (14.68) and high trust in science (58.39) produce clear support for labeling as a non-restrictive transparency measure.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely high trust in political institutions (94.22% mean) and very high agreeableness (trust 99.46%) generate substantial trust in mainstream journalism, moderated by low openness to conventional narratives.
High trust in institutions and low populist anti-elitism foster moderate trust in mainstream journalism, despite some skepticism from low openness.

Extremely low trust in science (2.57) and government (7.77) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite, out-of-touch institution.
Very low trust in science/experts, high anti-elitism, and low openness produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite institution.

Very low trust in institutions (6.19), high anti-elitism (78.31), and strong populist anti-establishment views (78.31) combined with low agreeableness (13.42) produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism.

Very low agreeableness, high collective narcissism, and strong anti-elitism combined with low openness and moderate institutional trust produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism, viewing it as a competing out-group voice.
Clear distrust stems from very low trust in institutions, high anti-elitism, and a competitive-jungle worldview that views mainstream journalism as part of the elite establishment.

High anti-elitism (79.77) and moderate trust in institutions (49.57) combined with low collective narcissism (0.79) produce mild distrust, though high conventionalism (88.47) prevents strong rejection.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely high trust in political system and institutions (94.22% political trust mean) and very high system justification (81.62%) produce strong opposition to mistrusting elections, consistent with a fundamentally trusting worldview.
Extremely high system justification and trust in government, combined with low populist attitudes, produce strong opposition to distrusting elections.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.
Very low trust in government, high authoritarian aggression, and a dangerous worldview fuel clear distrust in electoral integrity, seeing it as compromised by elites.

Extremely low trust in government (16.53), judiciary (8.26), and police (13.22) combined with very low political efficacy (3.69) and high collective narcissism (92.44) produce near-maximal distrust in electoral integrity.

Moderate trust in government and political system combined with high system justification and moderate external efficacy buffer against election distrust, but low openness and collective narcissism allow some skepticism to persist.
Strong distrust is consistent with very low political trust, high anti-elitism, low system justification, and a competitive-jungle worldview that sees elections as rigged by elites.

High conventionalism (88.47) and respect for rules (99.48) combined with moderate trust in government (55.44) and low dangerous worldview (14.68) produce clear disagreement with election distrust, though anti-elitism (79.77) prevents maximal rejection.
